coredata.dc:title: Histamine detection in fish samples based on indirect competitive ELISA method using iron-cobalt co-doped carbon dots labeled histamine antibody
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yi-Fang
Lin, Zheng-Zhong
Hong, Cheng-Yi
Huang, Zhi-Yong
coredata.dc:description: Due to complex matrixes and specific reagent deficiency, the rapid detection of histamine is still a challenge to date. Based on the high peroxidase-like activity of iron-cobalt co-doped carbon dots, an indirect competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ic-ELISA) was established for histamine detection using the mimic enzyme labeled with histamine antibody (His-Ab). Through the competitive binding of the labeled His-Ab to solid-phase and sample antigens, histamine content was detected with a linear range of 2.5–150 μg mL−1. The detection limit based on 3σ/K was 0.50 mg kg−1, which was much lower than those of commercial His-kit and HPLC methods. The ic-ELISA method was applied to histamine detection in fish samples with the recovery of (103.4 ± 0.5)%, which was in accord with those of commercial His-kit and HPLC methods. The results indicated that the established ic-ELISA method was suitable for rapid detection of histamine in fish samples with high accuracy, sensitivity and stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe&Co co-doped CDs
Mimic enzyme
ic-ELISA
Histamine
Fish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab
Preparation of His-hapten and coating antigen
Characterization of Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab and His-OVA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support came from the Foundation from the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province, China ( 2016Y0064 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China ( 2018J01432 ), National Key R and D Program of China ( 2018YFD0901003 ), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Xiamen, China ( 3502Z20183031 ), and the National Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship ( 2019xj577 , 2019xj592 and 2020xj089 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of histamine detection based on Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled ic-ELISA.
TEM (a), FT-IR (b), (c) XPS spectrum, and (d) EDS of Fe&Co co-doped CDs.
The reaction velocity of TMB and H2O2 catalyzed by Fe&Co co-doped CDs tested at different substrate concentrations
(a) UV absorption spectra of Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled His-Ab. (b) Standard working curve for histamine detection.
(a) Selectivity for histamine detection. (b) Histamine content in fish samples measured with the methods of ic-ELISA and HPLC, respectively. (ND means undetectable).
Adding standard recoveries of histamine in fish samples tested with Ic-ELISA and commercial His-kit (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND means undetectable. Data of inter-day are the detection values of three days.

head.ce:title: Histamine detection in fish samples based on indirect competitive ELISA method using iron-cobalt co-doped carbon dots labeled histamine antibody

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to complex matrixes and specific reagent deficiency, the rapid detection of histamine is still a challenge to date. Based on the high peroxidase-like activity of iron-cobalt co-doped carbon dots, an indirect competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ic-ELISA) was established for histamine detection using the mimic enzyme labeled with histamine antibody (His-Ab). Through the competitive binding of the labeled His-Ab to solid-phase and sample antigens, histamine content was detected with a linear range of 2.5–150 μg mL−1. The detection limit based on 3σ/K was 0.50 mg kg−1, which was much lower than those of commercial His-kit and HPLC methods. The ic-ELISA method was applied to histamine detection in fish samples with the recovery of (103.4 ± 0.5)%, which was in accord with those of commercial His-kit and HPLC methods. The results indicated that the established ic-ELISA method was suitable for rapid detection of histamine in fish samples with high accuracy, sensitivity and stability.

ce:section.ce:para: Histamine (His), one of the biogenic amines, is the decarboxylation of histidine by exogenous decarboxylases mainly produced by a number of bacteria (Hallász, Baráth, Sarkadi &Wilhelm, 1994). As a “scombroid fish poisoning” in aquatic products, histamine may cause various toxicological symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, urticaria, etc (Colombo, Cattaneo, Confalonieri & Bernardi, 2018). Therefore, the maximum levels of histamine in foods have been set by many countries and organizations concerning its food safety. For example, European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) and the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) have suggested that histamine concentrations in fresh foods should be less than 50 mg kg−1 (Keow, Abu Bakar, Salleh, Heng, Wagiran & Bean, 2007). The World Health Organization (WHO) and FDA reminded that food-borne histamine may cause mild adverse health reaction at the level of 50–200 mg kg−1, and may have toxic symptoms at the level above 200 mg kg−1 (Danchuk et al., 2020). Due to complex matrixes and specific reagent deficiency, it is still a challenge to rapidly detect histamine in aquatic products at present. Chromatographic methods including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography (GC) and tandem mass spectrometry are commonly used to detect histamine content in foods, but these methods suffer from tedious sample extraction and purification, and need the complicated processes of derivation before or after column separation (Munir & Badri, 2020).

Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), with highly specific recognition and the high efficiency of enzymatic response, has been used to rapidly detect histamine content. As a small molecular antigen, histamine is usually measured based on the method of indirect competitive ELISA (ic-ELISA), in which the coating antigens and sample antigens of histamine competitively combine with the labeled biologic antibodies (Xu, Zhou, Eremin, Dias & Zhang, 2020). However, the labeled natural enzymes (such as horse radish peroxidase (Durrant & Stone, 1996), alkaline phosphatase (Bozkurt, Buyukgoz, Soforoglu, Tamer, Suludere & Boyaci, 2018) and glucose oxidase (Geoghegan, Williams & Jordon, 1986), etc.) that are commonly used in ELISA have many disadvantages including instability, complex preparation and high cost (Niculescu et al., 2001).

Carbon dots (CDs), with particle sizes ranging from 1 to 10 nm, have been found to exhibit the peroxidase-like activity that can catalyze the reaction of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), by which many colorimetric probes have been developed (Shi et al., 2011). In addition, the mimic enzyme activity can be further promoted when CDs are doped with heterogeneous elements, especially for the doping of transition elements (Wang, Liu, Wu, Chen, Weng & Li, 2018). The high mimic enzyme activities of transition elements doped CDs may be attributed to the unoccupied molecular orbits of transition metals that can accept the lone pair electrons of oxygen in the catalytic substrate reactions, reducing the reaction activation energies (Bai, Chen & Zimmerman, 2018). Iron (Fe) and cobalt (Co) are two transition metals with abundant unoccupied molecular orbits, and are readily available metals with high stability and no toxicity. For this reason, Fe and Co were chosen as doping elements in the heterogeneous CDs synthesis in the present study. For further expanding the applications of the mimic enzymes, CDs have been conjugated to various recognition units such as antibodies (Zhang, Yu, Shi, Han, Guo & Liu, 2020), aptamers (Lu et al., 2016) and peptide fragments (Esteves Da Silva & Gonçalves, 2011) to improve the selectivity of the analytical targets. For example, CDs conjugated with antibodies were used as labeled enzymes in ELISA detections with many advantages such as high catalytic activities, good biocompatibilities, low costs and easy preservation compared with the commonly used natural biologic enzymes (Walther et al., 2020).

In the present study, Fe&Co co-doped CDs with high peroxidase-like activity was synthesized with one-step hydrothermal method, and was labeled with anti-His mouse monoclonal antibody (His-Ab). After coating the antigen of histamine on microwell plates, the histamine content was detected with the approach of ic-ELISA using Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab to competitively combine with the solid-phase antigens and sample antigens as shown in Fig. 1 . Based on the ic-ELISA, histamine content in fish samples were rapidly detected with high sensitivity and accuracy.

Anhydrous citric acid, iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), cobalt(II) chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), histamine dihydrochloride (C5H9N3·2HCl), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), p-formylbenzoic acid (C8H6O3), morpholine ethyl sulfonic acid (MES), dansyl chloride (Dns-Cl), triethylamine, and biogenic amines including putrescine (Put), cadaverine (Cad), spermine (Spm), spermidine (Spd), tyramine (Tyr), and tryptamine (Trp) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Trichloroacetic acid (TCA), acetonitrile and acetone were purchased from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shantou, China). All chemicals used in the study were analytical grade. Anti-His mouse monoclonal antibody (His-Ab) and ovalbumin (OVA) was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fish samples were purchased from the local supermarket in Xiamen, China.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM2010, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology and size of the Fe&Co co-doped CDs, and the surface functional groups of Fe&Co co-doped CDs were observed with Fourier infrared spectrum (FT-IR, Bruker ALPHA, Germany). The elemental component was measured with an Energy Dispersive System (EDS, Zeiss α300, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250xi, America). UV absorption spectra were carried out on a spectrophotometer (Lambda265, Perkin Elmer, America). The spectral absorbance of microwell plates was measured with a microplate reader (SpectraMax plus384, Molecular Devices, America).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis method of CDs was referred to a previous report with some modification (Wang, Liu, Wu, Chen, Weng & Li, 2018), and the preparation details of Fe&Co co-doped CDs was described in electronic Supporting materials (ESM). The Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab (as shown in Fig. S1) was prepared according to the references with some modifications (Dvorakova et al., 2017; Sam et al., 2010). Briefly, 10 mg of Fe&Co co-doped CDs was dispersed in 10 mL MES buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1, pH 5), and each of 15 mg of EDC and NHS was successively added under ultrasonic dispersing. The mixture was shaken at 37 °C for 15 min to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of Fe&Co co-doped CDs. Then, 100 μL of His-Ab (1:100 diluted with PBS) was added after the solution was adjusted to neutral pH value with triethylamine, and the solution was shaken at 37 °C overnight. The resulting solution was dialyzed with 800 Da of molecular weight cut-offfor 16 h, and the obtained Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of His-hapten and coating antigen
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S2, the synthesis of His-hapten was carried out by dissolving 2.76 g of histamine dihydrochloride in 15 mL of methanol in a round bottom flask. After subsequently adding 1.62 g of sodium methanol and 2.25 g of p-formylbenzoic acid, the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Then, 1.11 g of sodium borohydride was gently added and the reaction was sustained for 30 min until an opalescent solution was obtained. After purified with a 300 mesh silica gel column eluted with eluant containing chloroform, methanol and ammonia hydroxide at the volume ratio of 10:5:1, the collected eluent was mixed with ethanol and set at 4 °C overnight for the crystallization of His-hapten. After filtration, the crystals were washed with chilled ethanol for 3 times, and stored in 4 °C for use.

The coating antigen of His-OVA was prepared by dissolving 24.6 mg of His-hapten in 4 mL of boric acid (0.1 mol L−1, pH 4.5). Then, 38.4 mg of EDC and 180 mg of OVA were subsequently added at stirring state. The mixture was stirred at 4 °C overnight for His-OVA synthesis as shown in Fig. S3. Then, the mixture was dialyzed with 500 Da of molecular weight cut-offwith PBS (0.01 mol L−1, pH 7.4) at 4 °C for 3 d. After freeze-drying, the coating antigen of His-OVA was obtained and stored at −20 °C for further use.

His-OVA in CBS (0.1 mol L−1, pH 9.6) was coated on microwell plate at 100 μL well−1 at 4 °C overnight. Then, the plate was washed three times with PBS (0.01 mol L−1, pH 7.4) and dried. For blocking the unbinding sites, 0.5% (w/v) of skim milk was added in each of wells at 300 μL well−1 at 37 °C for 2 h. After washing three times with PBS, 50 μL of histamine standard solutions at different concentrations or sample solutions and 50 μL of the Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab (1:5000 diluted with PBS) were subsequently added in each of wells. After incubation at 37 °C for 1 h, the plate was washed and dried again. Then, each of wells was respectively mixed with 80 μL of NaAc-HAc buffer solution (1 mmol L−1), 100 μL of H2O2 (1 mmol L−1) and 20 μL of TMB (5 mmol L−1), and the plate was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min again. The reaction was terminated by adding 2 mol L−1 of H2SO4 at 50 μL well−1, and the absorbance values were measured at 450 nm using the microplate reader.

Samples were extracted by mixing 2.0 g of fish with 5 mL of extraction solution consisting of ethanol, methanol and water at the volume ratio of 5:25:75. After ultrasonic extraction for 20 min, the extraction solutions were centrifuged at 8 000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatants were collected and diluted with PBS for histamine measurement using ic-ELISA as described above. For verifying the accuracy of the ic-ELISA, histamine was also measured with HPLC using precolumn Dns-Cl derivatization method (Altieri, Semeraro, Scalise, Calderari & Stacchini, 2016). In addition, the detection stability of ic-ELISA was tested by intra-day and inter-day recoveries, respectively. And the performance of ic-ELISA was also compared with a commercial kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab and His-OVA
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized Fe&Co co-doped CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the morphology of Fe&Co co-doped CDs is spherical with an average diameter of 2.14 nm calculated from more than 100 particles. The crystal lattice on the surface of Fe&Co co-doped CDs can be clearly visible by zooming in the image and the lattice gap is 0.2 nm. FT-IR spectroscopy of Fe&Co co-doped CDs in Fig. 2(b) shows that there is a strong absorption peak at 1705 cm−1 which is generated by CO stretching vibration. The wide and scattered stretching vibration absorption peaks at 3500–2500 cm−1 may be attributed to the OH stretching vibration peaks in dimer carboxyl groups due to hydrogen bonding association, and the absorption peak at 1373 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of OH plane. The FT-IR implies that the synthesized Fe&Co co-doped CDs have abundant carboxyl groups on the surface, which provides the possibility of the immunoconjugate preparation using EDC/NHS reaction. The XPS and EDS in Fig. 2(c) and (d) show that Fe and Co are co-doped at the mass ratio of 1:1 in Fe&Co co-doped CDs.

The kinetics of TMB and H2O2 reaction catalyzed by Fe&Co co-doped CDs was tested as shown in Fig. 3 , in which the mimic enzyme shows good substrate affinity and high reaction velocity with km values of 102 µmol L−1 and 260 µmol L−1 to H2O2 and TMB, respectively.

Fig. 4 (a) shows that the Fe&Co co-doped CDs labeled-His-Ab has two UV absorption bands locating at 280 nm and 217 nm, respectively. The spectral peak of 280 nm might derive from the His-Ab, similar to general antibodies due to the aromatic groups in proteins (Gan, Du, Cao, Hu, Li & Jiang, 2013), and the strong peak of 217 nm might derive from the Fe&Co co-doped CDs rather than His-Ab because the adjacent peak locating at 210 nm which is due to the absorption of peptide bonds in antibody is weak (Bailey & Nobel, 2009; Gan, Du, Cao, Hu, Li & Jiang, 2013). The UV spectra in Fig. 4(a) indicate that the Fe&Co co-doped CDs has been successfully conjugated to His-Ab.

As a carrier protein, ovalbumin (OVA) was selected to couple with His-hapten to form His-OVA. Compared with BSA, OVA is more hydrophobic and might provide more binding sites of amino (–NH2) to couple with carboxyl (–COOH) of the synthesized His-hapten through EDC reagent, facilitating the synthesis of coating His-OVA. In addition, the immunoconjugate of BSA-His is usually used to prepare histamine monoclonal antibody through mouse host, which may produce BSA-antibodies and result in interferences when BSA is used as coating carrier protein. As shown in Fig. S4, His-OVA shows two UV spectral peaks at 210 nm and 280 nm, while His-hapten shows only one peak at 213 nm, which might be due to the spectral absorbance of imidazole ring in histamine molecule. After conjugation, the UV spectra of His-OVA show two absorption peaks at 220 nm and 280 nm, respectively. Compared with that of OVA, the peak of 220 nm for His-OVA is slightly red shift which might be due to the amidation reaction between histamine and OVA by means of EDC. The results prove that the His-hapten has been successfully conjugated to the carrier protein of OVA.

A relative inhibition rate (IR) of histamine in ic-ELISA measurement was calculated by the absorbance values with (A) and without (A0) histamine in samples according to Eq. (1).
(1)
IR
( % )
=
A
0
- A
A
0
× 100 %

Using Fe&Co co-doped CDs as labeling-enzyme of His-Ab, the linear equation of the ic-ELISA method was established between the IR(%) values and the histamine concentrations as shown in Fig. 4(b). A linear range from 2.5 μg mL−1 to 150 μg mL−1 was obtained with a linear equation of IR (%) = 0.3704C His + 3.7033 (R 2 = 0.994). The sensitivity of ic-ELISA for histamine detection was 106 μg mL−1 based on IC 50 value (i.e., the histamine concentration at 50% of IR).

The cross-reactivity (CR%) of the histamine measurement was tested with various biogenic amines including His, Put, Cad, Spm, Spd, Tyr and Trp, and the CR% value was calculated with Eq. (2). As shown in Fig. 5 (a), high IR% values of histamine ranging from 5 μg mL−1 to 1000 μg mL−1 were observed compared with those of other biogenic amines. Except for histamine with 100% of CR (%), the CR (%) values of the other biogenic amines were all less than 0.01% (Table S1), indicating that the ic-ELISA method presents good specificity for histamine detection.
(2)
CR
%
=
IR 50
His
IR 50
other
BAs
× 100 %

Histamine content in sixteen fish samples, including three kinds of chilled fish (No.1–3) and thirteen kinds of fermented fish (No.4–16), were detected with the established ic-ELISA method. And the results were verified with the method of HPLC. The results in Fig. 5(b) show that the histamine content were mostly less than 50 mg kg−1, the limitation of histamine in fresh foods set by EFSA and FDA. However, high levels of histamine were found in various fermented fish samples. For example, 208.4 mg kg−1 of histamine was observed in 13th sample which was a flavor fermented fish processed by a joint fermentation of fish, pork and rice.

Data in Fig. 5b show that the results of the ic-ELISA and HPLC methods are not significant difference (>0.05) tested with paired T-test by IBM SPSS Statistics 25 (as shown in Table S2). The results indicate that the detection of histamine in fish samples with the ic-ELISA method is feasible.

For further verifying the accuracy of the ic-ELISA method, the recoveries were tested by spiking histamine standards with three levels, and a commercial His-kit with natural enzyme (horse radish peroxidase, HRP) labeled-His-Ab was simultaneously used for comparison. Results in Table 1 show that the average recovery (103.4%) of ic-ELISA is good agreement with that (109.6%) of commercial His-kit.

For the measurement stability and repetitiveness of the ic-ELISA, the recovery tests were carried out at intra-day and inter-day, respectively. Results in Table 1 show that the average recovery for inter-day is 102.3% which is good accord with that (103.4%) of intra-day. But the relative standard deviations (RSD) for inter-day are slightly higher than those of intra-day. The results confirmed that the detection method of histamine in fish samples by ic-ELISA is accurate and reliable.

The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) for histamine measurement by ic-ELISA were tested and calculated based on 3σ/K and 10σ/K, respectively (where σ is the standard deviation of nine blank samples and K is the slope of linear equation). The results in Table S3 show that the LOD and LOQ values are 0.5 mg kg−1 and 1.67 mg kg−1, respectively, which are much lower than those of HPLC method, and even lower than those of commercial His-kit. In addition, the linear range of ic-ELISA is wider than that of commercial His-kit, but is narrower than that of HPLC method. The results demonstrate that the ic-ELISA method can be used to rapidly detect histamine content in fish samples with high sensitivity.

The mimic enzyme of Fe&Co co-doped CDs was synthesized and labeled with His-Ab. Through the competitive binding of the mimic enzyme labeled-His-Ab to the coating His-OVA and histamine in samples, the ic-ELISA was established and successfully applied to the detection of histamine in fish samples. By mean of the strong peroxidase-like activity of Fe&Co co-doped CDs, the established ic-ELISA shows higher sensitivity and wider linear range compared with that of commercial His-kit. Based on the real sample detection, the ic-ELISA was proved to be useful for histamine detection in fish samples with high accuracy.

Financial support came from the Foundation from the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province, China (2016Y0064), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2018J01432), National Key R and D Program of China (2018YFD0901003), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Xiamen, China (3502Z20183031), and the National Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (2019xj577, 2019xj592 and 2020xj089).
Fish samples were collected by Yi-Fang Li at local farmers' market, and no special collecting permit or “Animal Care Protocol” was required at that time.
Informed consent not applicable.

Yi-Fang Li: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Zheng-Zhong Lin: Investigation, Validation. Cheng-Yi Hong: Investigation, Validation. Zhi-Yong Huang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.128812.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
